front: Composer: at age 14, legendarily transcribed Allegri's Miserere in its entirety after two hearings
Composer: the 'Mozart effect' — a 1993 paper found that listening to the Sonata for Two Pianos in D major (K. 448) temporarily improved spatial reasoning; media exaggerated it as a magical IQ booster
Composer: collaborated with librettist Lorenzo Da Ponte on Don Giovanni, The Marriage of Figaro, and Così fan tutte; Così fan tutte is the third and last of these collaborations
Composer: A Musical Joke is a parody of incompetent composers; features out-of-place whole-tone scales and polytonality in the violin; scored for two horns and string quartet
Composer: four horn concertos dedicated to Joseph Leutgeb; wrote the most-performed bassoon concerto (in B-flat); wrote a Concerto for Flute and Harp (K. 299) for a father-daughter pair met in Paris

Symphony: finale combines five themes in a culminating five-voice fugato; the first theme is the whole notes C–D–F–E; first movement quotes the aria 'Un bacio di mano'; written in summer 1788 alongside symphonies No. 39 and No. 40
back: Symphony No. 41 in C major ("Jupiter") – Mozart
work: Symphony No. 41 in C major ("Jupiter") – K. 551

front: Symphony: nickname probably given by Johann Peter Salomon; Elaine Sisman wrote a book-length study of it; finale may have been inspired by Michael Haydn's Symphony No. 39/28
back: Symphony No. 41 in C major ("Jupiter") – Mozart
work: Symphony No. 41 in C major ("Jupiter") – K. 551

front: Symphony: opens with Sturm und Drang syncopated figure (G, down to D, up to E-flat, down to F-sharp); first movement opens the film Amadeus
back: Symphony No. 25 in G minor ("Little G minor") – Mozart
work: Symphony No. 25 in G minor – K. 183 ("Little G minor")

front: Requiem: left unfinished at Mozart's death; completed by Franz Xaver Süssmayr; 'Confutatis maledictis' immediately precedes the Lacrimosa
Requiem: seven-measure intro opens with bassoons playing D–C-sharp–D–E–F before the basset horns join; the double-fugue Kyrie references a German arrangement of Handel's Messiah
Sonata: best known for the third-movement Rondo alla Turca (opens with B–A–G-sharp–A landing on C); first-movement theme used by Max Reger for his Variations and Fugue
back: Piano Sonata No. 11 in A major (K. 331) – Mozart
work: Piano Sonata No. 11 in A major – K. 331

front: Concerto: last completed work; written for Anton Stadler ('Notschibinitschibi') and his basset clarinet (extended range instrument built by Theodor Lotz); published posthumously by Johann André
back: Clarinet Concerto in A major (K. 622) – Mozart
work: Clarinet Concerto in A major – K. 622

front: Opera: libretto by Lorenzo Da Ponte; Figaro schemes with Susanna against Count Almaviva; Cherubino (trouser role) sings 'Voi che sapete' while Susanna accompanies on guitar; 'Se vuol ballare' mocks the Count
work: The Marriage of Figaro – K. 492 (Le nozze di Figaro)

front: Opera: the statue of the Commendatore drags the title character to hell at a feast; ball scene has three ensembles playing three different dances simultaneously
Opera: singspiel with libretto by Emanuel Schikaneder, who also played Papageno at the premiere; inspired Tippett's Jungian Midsummer Marriage
Opera: Ferrando and Guglielmo disguise as Albanians to test their fiancées; the maid Despina dresses as a male doctor and uses a large magnet; last Da Ponte opera
Mass: unfinished; 'Et incarnatus est' is the last section Mozart wrote; reused in the cantata Davide penitente
back: Great Mass in C minor (K. 427) – Mozart
work: Great Mass in C minor – K. 427

front: Concerto: 27 total; named examples include 'Jeunehomme' (for Victoire Jenamy), 'Coronation' (for Leopold II's crowning), and 'Elvira Madigan'; Cuthbert Girdlestone categorized their slow movements as 'galant, romance, dream, meditative, and minor'
